subject: math
text:
6.1
Introduction to Vectors
Scalars
Deﬁnition: Measurements that have magnitude only. Scalar variables are written a, b, c, . . .
Examples: distance, height, speed, mass, time, temperature, . . .
Vectors
Deﬁnition: Measurements that have magnitude and direction. Vector variables are written ωa, ωb, ωc, . . .
Examples: velocity (10 m/s [N]), acceleration (2 m/s2 [NE]), force (50 N [fwd]), electrical currents (direction = along
the wire), magnetic ﬁelds, momentum, electric ﬁelds, displacement, torque, . . .
Two ways to describe a vector
• Geometric: drawn w.r.t. space/shape/orientation (e.g. N/E/S/W, or “moving forward”).
• Algebraic: expressed using components on a standard Cartesian grid.
Notation & Magnitude
→→↑
AB = vector from point A to point B.
→→→↑
AB = vector from B to A, i.e. →→→↑
AB = →→↑
BA.

|ωv|
or
↓ωv↓

If ωv is a vector, →ωv has the same size but opposite direction.
Equal Vectors & Location Independence
Key rule: Any vector with the same magnitude and direction is equal - location does not matter.

Vector Addition (Tip-to-Tail Rule)
To add two vectors, draw them tip-to-tail: ωv + ωu is the vector from the tail of ωv to the tip of ωu.

Basis & Algebraic Vectors
Basis = the directions that deﬁne a space. Algebraic vectors are given by their components along basis directions.
• 1D: basis is the concept of “1”; every number on a number line is relative to it.
• 2D: choose two directions that reach any point. Standard: x-direction ωı = (1, 0) and y-direction ωε = (0, 1) on the
xy-grid.
• 3D: uses ωı,ωε, and ωk directions.
Standard deﬁnitions: ωı = (1, 0),
ωε = (0, 1).
Any vector →↑
OA is expressed in its basis components:
→↑
OA = 2ωı + 3ωε
where 2 and 3 are the components of →↑
OA.
Non-standard basis (ωı→,ωε→rotated/skewed): the same geo-
metric vector gets di!erent components, e.g.
→↑
OA = 2.5ωı→→3.5ωε→

6.2
Vector Addition
Recall: Geometric vectors are deﬁned by their size and direction using systems such as [NESW] or simply drawing points
on a page and connecting them.
Algebraic Vectors have their size and direction deﬁned by the Cartesian Plane (x, y) coordinates. The position of the tip
(x, y) deﬁnes both the size and direction of the vector with the tail at the origin.
Vector addition is an operation we can perform on vectors that represents the result of two vectors positioned tip-to-tail.
Property: ωa +ωb = ωb + ωa
→Commutative
Proof:

Algebraic Vectors - Geo/Alg Conversion
Algebraic vectors are written most commonly using the x and y components from the Cartesian grid. Geometric vectors
have size and direction. We can convert from one to the other if we overlay the [NESW] system on the Cartesian grid.

2. Draw the vector with components to make a right triangle.

6.3
Multiplication of a Vector by a Scalar
Multiplication of a Vector by a Scalar
• The direction is not changed
• Only the size of the vector is changed
In general, vectors are not “multiplied”, but it is okay to multiply a vector by a scalar. However, we cannot add a scalar
and vector.
Consider ωa + ωa = 2ωa
Property: |kωa| = k|ωa|,
k →R, k ↑0
Ex:
a) ωa = 5ωn [N30→W], then 3ωa = 15ωn [N30→W]
b) ωa = (↓1, ↓6), then 3ωa = 3(↓1, ↓6) = (↓1, ↓6) + (↓1, ↓6) + (↓1, ↓6) = (3 ↔(↓1), 3 ↔(↓6)) = (↓3, ↓18)
If ωa = (ax, ay), then kωa = (kax, kay)
Deﬁnition: Collinear Vectors
Two vectors ωa and ωb are said to be collinear if they are in the same or opposite directions.
In other words, ωa and ωb are collinear if one vector can be written as a scalar multiple of the other.
If ωa = kωb for some k →R, then ωa and ωb are collinear.
Think: Must be parallel
Ex:
1) (2, ↓3) and (↓12, 18) are collinear because (2, ↓3) = ↓1
6(↓12, 18), or ↓6(2, ↓3) = (↓12, 18)
2) (1, 2) and (2, 3) are not collinear.

= (kax, kay) + (kbx, kby)
= (kax + kbx, kay + kby)
= k(ax + bx, ay + by)
= k(ωa +ωb)

Deﬁnition: Unit Vector
A unit vector has a magnitude of 1 in a certain direction. (1, 0) is a unit vector and so is (0, 1). (1, 1) is not a unit vector
because the magnitude of (1, 1) is
≃
12 + 12 =
≃
2 and the magnitude is obviously not 1.
Let ωa be any vector of any size in any direction. Then ˆa is the unit vector in the direction of ωa, and:
ˆa = 1

Drawing a 3-D Point or Vector
To draw a vector or point in 3D, we need to use the perspective of a 3D box.
x
y
z
To draw a 3D point or vector:
1. Mark the x, y, and z values on their axis.
2. Draw a rectangle to join the y and z value.
3. Draw parallelograms to join x and z and x and y.
4. Complete the box with the missing corner (which is your 3D point).
Octants and Coordinate Planes
Graph the point A(3, 2, 4).
